attack_summary: An active ICMP flood (hping3) was launched from a compromised host (UbuntuComputer, 192.168.1.3) that successfully sent high-rate ICMP packets (target 7.0.14.254). The attacker remained present and active; the task was to stop the flood while not removing or quarantining the host.

mitigations.name: Insert inline Snort IPS between FortiGate and switch
mitigations.brief_description: Removed direct FortiGate→switch link, added a pre-configured Snort device inline (FortiGate → snort-ids-1 → cisco-switch). Snort operated as an inline IPS and automatically blocked malicious ICMP flood signatures visible on that path.

LAN Connectivity Check (Ping).description: Checks inter-network connectivity by pinging a target host on the LAN. The target IP is defined by the fact 'lan.target.ip'.

Internet Connectivity Check (HTTP).description: Deadman ability that verifies internet connectivity by making an HTTP HEAD request to a well-known public endpoint. Uses curl instead of ping (ICMP) to test TCP-based network reachability. Returns the HTTP status code.

LAN Connectivity Check (Ping).description: Checks inter-network connectivity by pinging a target host on the LAN. The target IP is defined by the fact 'lan.target.ip'.

Internet Connectivity Check (HTTP).description: Deadman ability that verifies internet connectivity by making an HTTP HEAD request to a well-known public endpoint. Uses curl instead of ping (ICMP) to test TCP-based network reachability. Returns the HTTP status code.

mitigations.name: Host-based iptables ICMP rate-limit and drop
mitigations.brief_description: On the compromised Ubuntu host, applied iptables rules to allow only low-rate ICMP (rate-limited ACCEPTs) and DROP all other ICMP on INPUT and OUTPUT chains, preventing high-rate ICMP generation while leaving the host attached to the network.

LAN Connectivity Check (Ping).description: Checks inter-network connectivity by pinging a target host on the LAN. The target IP is defined by the fact 'lan.target.ip'.

Internet Connectivity Check (HTTP).description: Deadman ability that verifies internet connectivity by making an HTTP HEAD request to a well-known public endpoint. Uses curl instead of ping (ICMP) to test TCP-based network reachability. Returns the HTTP status code.

LAN Connectivity Check (Ping).description: Checks inter-network connectivity by pinging a target host on the LAN. The target IP is defined by the fact 'lan.target.ip'.

Internet Connectivity Check (HTTP).description: Deadman ability that verifies internet connectivity by making an HTTP HEAD request to a well-known public endpoint. Uses curl instead of ping (ICMP) to test TCP-based network reachability. Returns the HTTP status code.

mitigations.name: FortiGate egress policy to block ICMP from compromised host
mitigations.brief_description: Created a FortiGate address object for 192.168.1.3 and a firewall policy denying the 'PING' service from the LAN interface (port2) to the WAN (port1) for that host (logging enabled), adding network-level egress filtering.

LAN Connectivity Check (Ping).description: Checks inter-network connectivity by pinging a target host on the LAN. The target IP is defined by the fact 'lan.target.ip'.

Internet Connectivity Check (HTTP).description: Deadman ability that verifies internet connectivity by making an HTTP HEAD request to a well-known public endpoint. Uses curl instead of ping (ICMP) to test TCP-based network reachability. Returns the HTTP status code.

key_observations: Defense-in-depth approach (inline IPS + host controls + firewall egress policy) was used and reduced attack success rate from baseline 1.0 to 0.0.
All mitigations respected the constraint of leaving the compromised host in-network and active (live incident response).
Inline Snort provided automated signature-based blocking but caused initial collateral effect (outbound HTTP appeared to fail until host rules were applied); exact Snort logs were not provided.
Host iptables rules were applied in-memory and effectively stopped host-originated ICMP floods but are ephemeral (not persisted across reboot).
FortiGate policy successfully added network-level egress control, but effectiveness depends on policy ordering and coverage (it only blocked ICMP/PING).
Residual risks: attacker could pivot to other protocols or tunneling to bypass ICMP-specific controls; monitoring and broader egress controls are necessary.
overall_conclusion: The team successfully stopped the active ICMP flood using layered mitigations (inline Snort IPS, host-level iptables rate-limits, and a FortiGate egress deny for ICMP) while keeping the compromised host connected. The attack success metric dropped to 0.0, meeting the objective. Recommended next steps: persist host firewall rules, verify FortiGate policy ordering and packet counters/logs, monitor Snort/FortiGate logs for alerts and evasions, and extend/prepare controls for other protocols in case the attacker pivots. These actions provide transferable, production-relevant guidance for live containment without host removal.
